title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl package R tool estimating symmetric asymmetric Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) Nonlinear ARDL (NARDL) models, designed econometricians researchers analyzing cointegration dynamic relationships time series data. offers flexible model specifications, allowing users include deterministic variables, asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, trend components. package supports customizable lag structures, model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), parallel processing computational efficiency. Key features include: Flexible Formula Specification: Use Asymmetric(), Lasymmetric(), Sasymmetric() model asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics, deterministic() dummy variables. Lag Optimization: Choose automatic lag selection ("quick", "grid", "grid_custom") user-defined lags. Dynamic Analysis: Compute long-run coefficients, perform cointegration tests (PSS F, PSS t, Narayan), ECM estimation. vignette demonstrates use kardl() function estimate asymmetric ARDL model, perform diagnostic tests, visualize results, using economic data Turkey.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: define model formula using R’s formula syntax, incorporating asymmetric effects deterministic variables. use asymmetric() variables short- long-run asymmetry, Lasymmetric() long-run asymmetry, Sasymmetric() short-run asymmetry, deterministic() fixed dummy variables. trend term includes linear time trend model. Indeed, formula syntax flexible, allowing various combinations asymmetric deterministic variables. following variations formula equivalent yield model specification:
code: # Define the model formula MyFormula <- CPI ~ ER + PPI + asymmetric(ER + PPI) + deterministic(covid) + trend sameFormula <- y ~Asymmetric(x1)+Sasymmetric(x2+x3)+Lasymmetric(x4+x5) + Deterministic(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~asymmetric(x1)+Sasymmetric(x2+x3)+Lasymmetric(x4+x5) + deterministic(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~asym(x1)+sasym(x2+x3)+lasym(x4+x5) + det(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~a(x1)+s(x2+x3)+l(x4+x5) + d(dummy1) + trend

text: "grid" mode evaluates lag combinations maxlag provides console feedback. Summary model provides detailed information estimated coefficients, standard errors, t-values, significance levels.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: LagCriteria component contains lag combinations criterion values. visualize compare model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, HQ).
code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2) # Convert LagCriteria to a data frame LagCriteria <- as.data.frame(kardl_model$lagInfo$LagCriteria) colnames(LagCriteria) <- c("lag", "AIC", "BIC", "AICc", "HQ") LagCriteria <- LagCriteria %>% mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC, HQ), as.numeric))  # Pivot to long format LagCriteria_long <- LagCriteria %>%   select(-AICc) %>%   pivot_longer(cols = c(AIC, BIC, HQ), names_to = "Criteria", values_to = "Value")  # Find minimum values min_values <- LagCriteria_long %>%   group_by(Criteria) %>%   slice_min(order_by = Value) %>%   ungroup()  # Plot ggplot(LagCriteria_long, aes(x = lag, y = Value, color = Criteria, group = Criteria)) +   geom_line() +   geom_point(data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value), color = "red", size = 3, shape = 8) +   geom_text(data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value, label = lag), vjust = 1.5, color = "black", size = 3.5) +   labs(title = "Lag Criteria Comparison", x = "Lag Configuration", y = "Criteria Value") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

text: calculate long-run coefficients using kardl_longrun(), standardizes coefficients dividing negative dependent variable’s long-run parameter. summary() function provides detailed information long-run coefficients, including standard errors, t-values, significance levels.

Error t value  Pr(>|t|)     ## L1.ER_POS   0.90921    0.20834  4.3640 1.587e-05 *** ## L1.ER_NEG   2.07904    0.56693  3.6672 0.0002747 *** ## L1.PPI_POS  4.25455    1.72822  2.4618 0.0141998 *   ## L1.PPI_NEG  3.71155    1.33518  2.7798 0.0056681 **  ## --- ## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ##  ## Note: ## Coefficients, standard errors, t-statistics and p-values are reliably estimated. ## Fitted values and residuals are NOT centered (E(u) ≠ 0 by design) → diagnostic plots and residual-based tests are invalid.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: symmetrytest() function performs Wald tests assess short- long-run asymmetry model. Summary symmetry test provides detailed results long-run short-run asymmetry tests, including F-values, p-values, hypotheses, test decisions.
code: ast <- imf_example_data %>% kardl(CPI ~ ER + PPI + asymmetric(ER + PPI) + deterministic(covid) + trend, mode = c(1, 2, 3, 0, 1)) %>% symmetrytest() ast ##  ## Symmetry Test Results - Long-run: ## ======================= ##     Df  Sum of Sq    Mean Sq F value Pr(>F) ## ER   1 0.00049756 0.00049756  2.1649 0.1419 ## PPI  1 0.00016439 0.00016439  0.7153 0.3982 ##  ## Symmetry Test Results - Short-run: ## ======================= ##     Df  Sum of Sq    Mean Sq F value Pr(>F) ## ER   1 0.00038607 0.00038607  1.6798 0.1956 ## PPI  1 0.00017426 0.00017426  0.7582 0.3844 # Summary of symmetry test summary(ast) ## Long-run symmetry tests: ##  ## Test for variable:  ER  ## F statistic: 2.164906, p-value: 0.1418984 ## Test Decision:  Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating long-run symmetry for variable ER.  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: - Coef(L1.ER_POS)/Coef(L1.CPI) = - Coef(L1.ER_NEG)/Coef(L1.CPI) ## H1: - Coef(L1.ER_POS)/Coef(L1.CPI) ≠ - Coef(L1.ER_NEG)/Coef(L1.CPI) ##  ## Test for variable:  PPI  ## F statistic: 0.7152717, p-value: 0.3981528 ## Test Decision:  Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating long-run symmetry for variable PPI.  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: - Coef(L1.PPI_POS)/Coef(L1.CPI) = - Coef(L1.PPI_NEG)/Coef(L1.CPI) ## H1: - Coef(L1.PPI_POS)/Coef(L1.CPI) ≠ - Coef(L1.PPI_NEG)/Coef(L1.CPI) ##  ##  ## _____________________________ ## Short-run symmetry tests: ##  ## Test for variable:  ER  ## F  statistic:  1.679811 , p-value:  0.1956198  ## Test Decision:  Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating short-run symmetry for variable ER.  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L0.d.ER_POS) + Coef(L1.d.ER_POS) + Coef(L2.d.ER_POS) = Coef(L0.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L2.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L3.d.ER_NEG) ## H1: Coef(L0.d.ER_POS) + Coef(L1.d.ER_POS) + Coef(L2.d.ER_POS) ≠ Coef(L0.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L2.d.ER_NEG) + Coef(L3.d.ER_NEG) ##  ## Test for variable:  PPI  ## F  statistic:  0.7582018 , p-value:  0.3843603  ## Test Decision:  Fail to Reject H0 at 5% level. Indicating short-run symmetry for variable PPI.  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L0.d.PPI_POS) = Coef(L0.d.PPI_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.PPI_NEG) ## H1: Coef(L0.d.PPI_POS) ≠ Coef(L0.d.PPI_NEG) + Coef(L1.d.PPI_NEG)

title: Introduction to kardl
text: pssf() function tests cointegration using Pesaran, Shin, Smith F Bound test. Summary PSS F Bound test provides detailed information test statistic, critical values, hypotheses, decision regarding cointegration.
code: A <- kardl_model %>% pssf(case = 3, signif_level = "0.05") A ##  ##  Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds F-test for cointegration ##  ## data:  model ## F = 11.271 ## alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists summary(A) ## Pesaran-Shin-Smith (PSS) Bounds F-test for cointegration  ## F  =  11.27056  ## k =  4  ##  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L1.CPI) = Coef(L1.ER_POS) = Coef(L1.ER_NEG) = Coef(L1.PPI_POS) = Coef(L1.PPI_NEG) = 0  ## H1: Coef(L1.CPI) ≠ Coef(L1.ER_POS) ≠ Coef(L1.ER_NEG) ≠ Coef(L1.PPI_POS) ≠ Coef(L1.PPI_NEG)≠ 0  ##  ## Test Decision:  Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 5% level)  ##  ## Critical Values (Case  V ): ##          L    U ## 0.10  3.03 4.06 ## 0.05  3.47 4.57 ## 0.025 3.89 5.07 ## 0.01  4.40 5.72 ##  ## Notes: ##    • Trend detected in the model. Case automatically adjusted to 5 (unrestricted intercept and trend).

text: narayan() function tailored small sample sizes. tests cointegration using critical values optimized small samples. Summary Narayan test provides detailed information test statistic, critical values, hypotheses, decision regarding cointegration.
code: A <- kardl_model %>% narayan(case = 3, signif_level = "0.05") A ##  ##  Narayan F Test for Cointegration ##  ## data:  model ## F = 11.271 ## alternative hypothesis: Cointegrating relationship exists summary(A) ## Narayan F Test for Cointegration  ## F  =  11.27056  ## k =  4  ##  ## Hypotheses: ## H0: Coef(L1.CPI) = Coef(L1.ER_POS) = Coef(L1.ER_NEG) = Coef(L1.PPI_POS) = Coef(L1.PPI_NEG) = 0  ## H1: Coef(L1.CPI) ≠ Coef(L1.ER_POS) ≠ Coef(L1.ER_NEG) ≠ Coef(L1.PPI_POS) ≠ Coef(L1.PPI_NEG)≠ 0  ##  ## Test Decision:  Reject H0 → Cointegration (at 5% level)  ##  ## Critical Values (Case  V ): ##          L     U ## 0.10 3.160 4.230 ## 0.05 3.678 4.840 ## 0.01 4.890 6.164 ##  ## Notes: ##    • The Narayan F-test is designed for small samples. Your model uses only 469 observations. For greater accuracy with large samples, consider pssf() function.  ##    • Trend detected in the model. Case automatically adjusted to 5 (unrestricted intercept and trend). ##    • The number of observations exceeds the maximum limit for the critical values table. Using the critical values for 80 observations.

title: Introduction to kardl
text: kardl(data, model, maxlag, mode, ...): data: time series dataset (e.g., data frame CPI, ER, PPI). asymmetric(): Asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics. Lasymmetric(): Long-run asymmetric variables. Sasymmetric(): Short-run asymmetric variables. deterministic(): Fixed dummy variables. trend: Linear time trend. maxlag: Maximum number lags (default: 4). Use smaller values (e.g., 2) small datasets, larger values (e.g., 8) long-term dependencies. "quick": Verbose output interactive use. "grid": Verbose output lag optimization. "grid_custom": Silent, efficient execution. User-defined vector (e.g., c(1, 2, 4, 5) c(CPI = 2, ER_POS = 3, ER_NEG = 1, PPI = 3)). Returns list components: inputs, finalModel, start_time, end_time, properLag, TimeSpan, OptLag, LagCriteria, type (“kardlmodel”). kardl_set(...): Configures options like criterion (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), differentAsymLag, AsymPrefix, Sasymuffix, ShortCoef, LongCoef. Use kardl_get() retrieve settings kardl_reset() restore defaults. kardl_longrun(model): Calculates standardized long-run coefficients, returning type (“kardl_longrun”), coef, delta_se, results, starsDesc. symmetrytest(model): Performs Wald tests short- long-run asymmetry, returning Lhypotheses, Lwald, Shypotheses, Swald, type (“symmetrytest”). pssf(model, case, signif_level): Performs Pesaran, Shin, Smith F Bound test cointegration, supporting cases 1–5 significance levels (“auto”, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.10). psst(model, case, signif_level): Performs PSS t Bound test, focusing lagged dependent variable’s coefficient. narayan(model, case, signif_level): Conducts Narayan test cointegration, optimized small samples (cases 2–5). ecm(data, model, maxlag, mode,  ...): Conducts Restricted ECM test cointegration, similar parameters kardl() case/significance level options. detailed documentation, use ?kardl, ?kardl_set, ?kardl_longrun, ?symmetrytest, ?pssf, ?psst, ?narayan, ?ecm.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: define model formula using R’s formula syntax, incorporating asymmetric effects deterministic variables. use asymmetric() variables short- long-run asymmetry, Lasymmetric() long-run asymmetry, Sasymmetric() short-run asymmetry, deterministic() fixed dummy variables. trend term includes linear time trend model. Indeed, formula syntax flexible, allowing various combinations asymmetric deterministic variables. following variations formula equivalent yield model specification:
code: # Define the model formula MyFormula <- CPI ~ ER + PPI + asymmetric(ER + PPI) + deterministic(covid) + trend sameFormula <- y ~Asymmetric(x1)+Sasymmetric(x2+x3)+Lasymmetric(x4+x5) + Deterministic(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~asymmetric(x1)+Sasymmetric(x2+x3)+Lasymmetric(x4+x5) + deterministic(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~asym(x1)+sasym(x2+x3)+lasym(x4+x5) + det(dummy1) + trend sameFormula <- y ~a(x1)+s(x2+x3)+l(x4+x5) + d(dummy1) + trend

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: LagCriteria component contains lag combinations criterion values. visualize compare model selection criteria (AIC, BIC, HQ).
code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2) # Convert LagCriteria to a data frame LagCriteria <- as.data.frame(kardl_model$lagInfo$LagCriteria) colnames(LagCriteria) <- c("lag", "AIC", "BIC", "AICc", "HQ") LagCriteria <- LagCriteria %>% mutate(across(c(AIC, BIC, HQ), as.numeric))  # Pivot to long format LagCriteria_long <- LagCriteria %>%   select(-AICc) %>%   pivot_longer(cols = c(AIC, BIC, HQ), names_to = "Criteria", values_to = "Value")  # Find minimum values min_values <- LagCriteria_long %>%   group_by(Criteria) %>%   slice_min(order_by = Value) %>%   ungroup()  # Plot ggplot(LagCriteria_long, aes(x = lag, y = Value, color = Criteria, group = Criteria)) +   geom_line() +   geom_point(data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value), color = "red", size = 3, shape = 8) +   geom_text(data = min_values, aes(x = lag, y = Value, label = lag), vjust = 1.5, color = "black", size = 3.5) +   labs(title = "Lag Criteria Comparison", x = "Lag Configuration", y = "Criteria Value") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: kardl(data, model, maxlag, mode, ...): data: time series dataset (e.g., data frame CPI, ER, PPI). asymmetric(): Asymmetric effects short- long-run dynamics. Lasymmetric(): Long-run asymmetric variables. Sasymmetric(): Short-run asymmetric variables. deterministic(): Fixed dummy variables. trend: Linear time trend. maxlag: Maximum number lags (default: 4). Use smaller values (e.g., 2) small datasets, larger values (e.g., 8) long-term dependencies. "quick": Verbose output interactive use. "grid": Verbose output lag optimization. "grid_custom": Silent, efficient execution. User-defined vector (e.g., c(1, 2, 4, 5) c(CPI = 2, ER_POS = 3, ER_NEG = 1, PPI = 3)). Returns list components: inputs, finalModel, start_time, end_time, properLag, TimeSpan, OptLag, LagCriteria, type (“kardlmodel”). kardl_set(...): Configures options like criterion (AIC, BIC, AICc, HQ), differentAsymLag, AsymPrefix, Sasymuffix, ShortCoef, LongCoef. Use kardl_get() retrieve settings kardl_reset() restore defaults. kardl_longrun(model): Calculates standardized long-run coefficients, returning type (“kardl_longrun”), coef, delta_se, results, starsDesc. symmetrytest(model): Performs Wald tests short- long-run asymmetry, returning Lhypotheses, Lwald, Shypotheses, Swald, type (“symmetrytest”). pssf(model, case, signif_level): Performs Pesaran, Shin, Smith F Bound test cointegration, supporting cases 1–5 significance levels (“auto”, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.10). psst(model, case, signif_level): Performs PSS t Bound test, focusing lagged dependent variable’s coefficient. narayan(model, case, signif_level): Conducts Narayan test cointegration, optimized small samples (cases 2–5). ecm(data, model, maxlag, mode, ...): Conducts Restricted ECM test cointegration, similar parameters kardl() case/significance level options. detailed documentation, use ?kardl, ?kardl_set, ?kardl_longrun, ?symmetrytest, ?pssf, ?psst, ?narayan, ?ecm.

text: data parameter essential kardl() function perform model estimation. contain variables specified model formula, including dependent variable independent variables, asymmetric components, deterministic variables defined formula. trend generated automatically specified formula. Input data can form data frame, tibble, time series object (e.g., ts, xts, zoo). providing data, ensure : - dataset clean free missing values variables used model. - variables appropriately formatted (e.g., numeric continuous variables). - time series data ordered correctly, especially analysis involves lagged variables.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: formula formula object specifying model estimated. default value NULL, means user must provide model formula calling kardl() function.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: model parameter defines structure ARDL NARDL model estimated. include dependent variable left side formula independent variables, asymmetric components, deterministic variables, trend (applicable) right side. formula can include: - Asymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects short- long -run dynamics. - Lasymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects long-run -dynamics. - Sasymmetric(): specify variables asymmetric effects short-run -dynamics. - Deterministic(): include fixed dummy variables (e.g., seasonal d -ummies, event dummies). - trend: include linear time trend model. constructing model formula, ensure : - variables used formula present data provided. - formula syntactically correct follows R’s formula conventions. - use asymmetric deterministic functions appropriate research question data characteristics.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: maxlag integer value specifying maximum number lags considered model estimation. default value 4.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: maxlag parameter sets upper limit number lags kardl() function evaluate optimizing lag structure model. particularly important using modes like "grid" "grid_custom", function systematically tests different lag combinations specified maximum. choosing value maxlag, consider following: - Data Frequency: monthly data, maxlag 4 often sufficient capture short-term dynamics. quarterly data, lower maxlag ( e.g., 2) may appropriate, daily data, higher maxlag (e.g., 8 ) might necessary. - Sample Size: larger maxlag increases number parameters estimate, can problematic small sample sizes. Ensure sample size adequate support number lags considered. - Model Complexity: Higher maxlag values lead complex models, may overfit data. Balance need capturing dynamics risk overfitting. - Computational Resources: Evaluating large number lag combinations can computationally intensive. Consider available resources time constraints setting maxlag.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: mode character string numeric vector specifying mode model estimation. default value "quick". available options : - “quick”: mode provides fast estimation model without optimizing lags. suitable initial explorations user predefined lag structure. - “grid”: mode performs grid search possible lag combinations specified maxlag. provides verbose output, including lag criteria combination, useful thorough lag optimization. - “grid_custom”: Similar "grid", silent execution. efficient large datasets user wants avoid console output lag optimization process. - User-defined vector: user can specify custom lag structure providing numeric vector (e.g., c(1, 2, 4, 5)) named vector (e.g., c(CPI = 2, ER_POS = 3, ER_NEG = 1, PPI = 3)). allows complete control lag selection process.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: mode parameter determines kardl() function approaches estimation ARDL NARDL model. mode advantages suited different scenarios: - Use "quick" rapid assessments lag structure already known computational speed priority. - Use "grid" comprehensive lag optimization, especially optimal lag structure unknown. mode ideal exploratory analysis model selection. - Use "grid_custom" efficient lag optimization without console output, particularly large datasets running multiple models batch mode. - Use user-defined vector user specific knowledge appropriate lags variable, allowing tailored model specifications. using "grid" "grid_custom", ensure maxlag parameter set appropriately balance thoroughness search computational feasibility.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: criterion character string specifying criterion used selecting optimal lags. default value "AIC". available options : User defined function: detailed information model selection criteria used methods, see documentation modelCriterion function. AIC: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC). criterion balances model fit complexity, favoring models lower AIC value. widely used time series analysis model selection. BIC: Schwarz Criterion (SC), also known Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). criterion imposes stricter penalty model complexity compared AIC, often leading simpler models data size large. AICc: Corrected Akaike Information Criterion. modification AIC accounts small sample sizes. reliable AIC number observations limited. HQ: Hannan-Quinn Criterion. criterion similar AIC BIC uses logarithmic penalty term grows slowly BIC. often used econometric applications.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: detailed information model selection criteria used methods, see documentation modelCriterion function. choice criterion can significantly impact selected lag length , consequently, performance model. criterion strengths suited specific scenarios: Use "AIC" general purposes, especially prioritizing good fit simplicity. Use "BIC" prefer parsimonious model, particularly large datasets. Use "AICc" working small sample sizes avoid overfitting. Use "HQ" balance AIC BIC, often econometrics time series models. Ensure selected criterion aligns goals analysis characteristics data.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: Asymmetric decomposition separates variable positive negative changes. models, may desirable assign different lags components capture distinct dynamic behaviors. Setting differentAsymLag = TRUE allows function optimize lags positive negative components independently. differentAsymLag = FALSE, components share lag. parameter particularly useful : Positive negative changes variable expected different impacts time. user wants flexibility modeling asymmetric responses. Attention! differentAsymLag = TRUE, ensure model sufficient data estimate separate lags reliably. models limited observations high number variables, differentAsymLag = FALSE may robust computationally efficient.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: AsymPrefix character vector specifying prefixes used naming asymmetric variables created positive negative decomposition. default value empty vector c(), indicating prefixes added default. specified, prefixes added beginning variable names represent positive negative decomposition: first element vector corresponds positive decomposition. second element vector corresponds negative decomposition.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: Asymmetric decomposition used analyze separate effects positive negative changes variable. example, given variable X, prefixes can used generate POS_X NEG_X positive negative components, respectively. default, prefixes applied (AsymPrefix = c()). However, users can define custom prefixes providing vector two elements. example: kardl_set(AsymPrefix = c("POS_", "NEG_")) results variable names POS_X NEG_X. kardl_set(AsymPrefix = c("Increase_", "Decrease_")) results variable names Increase_X Decrease_X. Attention! vector must contain exactly two elements: first positive decomposition second negative decomposition. prefixes used combination suffixes (via AsymSuffix), ensure resulting variable names meaningful conflict.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: AsymSuffix character vector specifying suffixes used naming asymmetric variables created positive negative decomposition. default value c("_POS", "_NEG"), : "_POS" suffix appended variables representing positive decomposition. "_NEG" suffix appended variables representing negative decomposition.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: order suffixes important: first element vector corresponds positive decomposition. second element vector corresponds negative decomposition. Asymmetric decomposition commonly used models separate effects positive negative changes variable. example, given variable X, decomposition may result X_POS X_NEG represent positive negative components, respectively. default, suffixes "_POS" "_NEG" used, users can customize needed providing custom vector. example: AsymSuffix = c("_Increase", "_Decrease") results variable names X_Increase X_Decrease. AsymSuffix = c("_Up", "_Down") results variable names X_Up X_Down. Attention! first element must always represent positive decomposition. second element must always represent negative decomposition. Providing fewer elements incorrect ordering may cause errors variable naming.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: LongCoef character string specifying prefix format naming long-run coefficients model output. default value "L{lag}.{varName}", : {lag} placeholder lag number. {varName} placeholder variable name. format generates names like L1.X first lag variable X.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: Long-run coefficients represent long-term relationships dependent variable independent variables ARDL NARDL model. LongCoef parameter allows users customize coefficients named output, making easier identify interpret . default format "L{lag}.{varName}" widely used provides clear information lag variable associated coefficient. Users can modify format changing LongCoef string. example: LongCoef = "LongRun_{varName}_Lag{lag}" results names like LongRun_X_Lag1. LongCoef = "LR_{varName}_L{lag}" results names like LR_X_L1. Attention! Ensure placeholders {lag} {varName} included custom format maintain clarity coefficient names. Avoid using special characters spaces format may cause issues R variable naming conventions.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: ShortCoef character string specifying prefix format naming short-run coefficients model output. default value "L{lag}.d.{varName}", : {lag} placeholder lag number. {varName} placeholder variable name. format generates names like L1.d.X first lag differenced variable X.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: Short-run coefficients capture immediate effects changes independent variables dependent variable ARDL NARDL model. ShortCoef parameter allows users customize coefficients named output, facilitating easier identification interpretation. default format "L{lag}.d.{varName}" commonly used provides clear information lag, differencing, variable associated coefficient. Users can modify format changing ShortCoef string. example: ShortCoef = "ShortRun_{varName}_Lag{lag}" results names like ShortRun_X_Lag1. ShortCoef = "SR_{varName}_L{lag}" results names like SR_X_L1. Attention! Ensure placeholders {lag} {varName} included custom format maintain clarity coefficient names. Avoid using special characters spaces format may cause issues R variable naming conventions.

title: Make Symmetric and Asymmetric ARDL Estimations
text: batch parameter particularly useful dealing large datasets complex models require significant computational resources. specifying batch size, users can divide model estimation process smaller, manageable segments, can processed parallel. format batch parameter "m/n", : m number batches processed parallel. n total number batches. example, setting batch = "2/4" divide estimation 4 batches, 2 batches processed simultaneously. can significantly reduce computation time, especially models extensive lag structures large numbers variables. Attention! Ensure specified batch size appropriate available computational resources (e.g., CPU cores, memory). Overloading system many parallel processes may lead performance degradation failures. effectiveness batching may vary depending specific model dataset characteristics.

title: Estimate a Restricted ECM Model — ecm
text: `ecm` function estimates restricted Error Correction Model (ECM) based provided data model specification. function designed test cointegration using PSS t Bound test, assesses presence long-term equilibrium relationship dependent variable independent variables model.

title: Estimate a Restricted ECM Model — ecm
text: null alternative hypotheses restricted ECM test follows: $$\mathbf{H_{0}:} \theta  =   0$$   $$\mathbf{H_{1}:} \theta  \neq 0$$ null hypothesis (\(H_{0}\)) states cointegration model, meaning long-run relationship variables significant. alternative hypothesis (\(H_{1}\)) suggests cointegration, indicating significant long-term relationship variables. test statistic calculated t-statistic coefficient error correction term (\(\theta\)) ECM model. absolute value t-statistic exceeds critical value PSS t Bound table, reject null hypothesis favor alternative hypothesis, indicating cointegration present. cases restricted ECM Bound test defined follows: case 1: constant, trend. case used model include constant term trend term. suitable models variables stationary exhibit long-term trends. model specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta y_t =  \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1}  - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t \end{aligned} $$ case 2: Restricted constant, trend. case used model includes constant term trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship trend component.   model specified follows:   $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1} - \alpha_0 - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}   $$ case 3: Unrestricted constant, trend. case used model includes unrestricted constant term trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship constant trend component. model specified follows: $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1} - \alpha_0 - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}     $$ case 4: Unrestricted Constant, restricted trend. case used model includes unrestricted constant term restricted trend term. suitable models variables exhibit long-term relationship constant trend component. model specified follows: $$   \begin{aligned}   \Delta y_t &= \phi + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1} - \pi (t-1) - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t   \end{aligned}    $$ case 5: Unrestricted constant, unrestricted trend. Error Correction Model (ECM) specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta y_t &= \phi + \varphi t +  \sum_{j=1}^{p} \gamma_j \Delta y_{t-j} + \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=0}^{q_i} \beta_{ij} \Delta x_{,t-j} + \theta (y_{t-1}  - \sum_{=1}^{k} \alpha_i x_{,t-1} ) + e_t \end{aligned} $$

title: Estimate a Restricted ECM Model — ecm
text: reported coefficients, prefix L denotes lagged variables, accompanying number indicates lag order, .d. denotes first differences. Accordingly, L1.PPI represents first lag level PPI (long-run component), L3.d.PPI denotes third lag first-differenced PPI (short-run component). addition, suffixes _POS _NEG indicate positive negative partial sum components variable, respectively. notation used default remains valid unless modified kardl_set() function.

title: Estimate ARDL and NARDL Models with Automatic Lag Selection — kardl
text: function estimates Autoregressive Distributed Lag (ARDL) Nonlinear ARDL (NARDL) model based provided data model formula. allows flexible specification variables, including deterministic terms, asymmetric variables, trend components. function also supports automatic lag selection using various information criteria.

Example formula:        y ~ x + z + Asymmetric(z) + Lasymmetric(x2 + x3) + Sasymmetric(x3 + x4) + deterministic(dummy1 + dummy2) + trend Details formula allows flexible specification variables roles: Deterministic variables: Deterministic regressors (e.g., dummy variables) can included using deterministic(). Multiple deterministic variables may supplied using +, example deterministic(dummy1 + dummy2). variables treated fixed components associated short-run long-run dynamics. Asymmetric variables: Asymmetric decompositions can specified short-run /long-run dynamics: Sasymmetric: Specifies short-run asymmetric variables. example, Sasymmetric(x1 + x2) applies short-run asymmetric decomposition x1 x2. Lasymmetric: Specifies long-run asymmetric variables. example, Lasymmetric(x1 + x3) applies long-run asymmetric decomposition x1 x3. Asymmetric: Specifies variables enter short-run long-run asymmetric decompositions. example, Asymmetric(x1 + x3) applies asymmetric decomposition dynamics. trend term may included capture deterministic linear time trends simply adding trend formula. formula also supports use . represent available regressors supplied data (excluding dependent variable), following standard R formula conventions. operators Deterministic(), Sasymmetric(), Lasymmetric(), Asymmetric() follow usage rules: can freely combined within single formula, example: must nested within one another. Valid usage:   y ~ x + deterministic(dummy) + Asymmetric(z).   Invalid usage (avoided):   y ~ x + deterministic(Asymmetric(z))   y ~ x + Asymmetric(deterministic(dummy)). applicable, arguments validated internally using   match.arg(). Consequently, abbreviated inputs accepted   provided uniquely identify valid option. example,   "asymmetric" admissible value, specifying ""   sufficient. clarity reproducibility, however, full   argument names recommended. components may therefore combined flexibly construct specification tailored empirical analysis. maxlag integer specifying maximum number lags considered model.        default value 4. parameter sets upper limit lag length        model estimation process. details maxlag parameter crucial defining maximum lag length model        evaluate selecting optimal lag structure based specified criterion.        controls computational effort helps prevent overfitting restricting search        space lag selection. data short time horizon prone overfitting, consider reducing maxlag. data expected long-term dependencies, increasing maxlag may necessary capture relevant dynamics. Setting appropriate value maxlag depends nature dataset        context analysis: small datasets quick tests, use smaller values (e.g., maxlag = 2). datasets observations longer-term patterns, larger values (e.g., maxlag = 8)  may appropriate, though increases computational time. examples Using default maximum lag (4) kardl(data, MyFormula, maxlag = 4) Reducing maximum lag 2 faster computation kardl(data, MyFormula, maxlag = 2) Increasing maximum lag 8 datasets longer dependencies kardl(data, MyFormula, maxlag = 8) mode Specifies mode estimation output control. parameter determines        function handles lag estimation kind feedback control provided        process. available options : "quick" (default):         Displays progress messages console function estimates optimal lag values.         mode suitable interactive use users want monitor estimation process         real-time. provides detailed feedback debugging observation may use additional         resources due verbose output. "grid" :         Displays progress messages console function estimates optimal lag values.         mode suitable interactive use users want monitor estimation process         real-time. provides detailed feedback debugging observation may use additional         resources due verbose output. "grid_custom":         Suppresses console output, prioritizing faster execution reduced resource usage         PCs servers. mode recommended high-performance scenarios, batch processing,         estimation process require user monitoring. Suitable large-scale         repeated runs output unnecessary. User-defined vector:         numeric vector lag values specified user, allowing full customization lag         structure used model estimation. user-defined vector provided (e.g., `c(1, 2, 4, 5)`),         function skips lag optimization process directly uses specified lags. Users can define lag values directly numeric vector. example:           mode = c(1, 2, 4, 5) assigns lags 1, 2, 4, 5 variables specified order.          Alternatively, lag values can assigned variables name clarity control. example:           mode = c(CPI = 2, ER_POS = 3, ER_NEG = 1, PPI = 3) assigns lags variables explicitly.          Ensure lags correctly designated verifying result using           kardl_model$properLag estimation. Attention!         function-based criterion user-defined function can specified          model selection, supported mode = "grid_custom"          mode = "quick". mode = "grid" option restricted          predefined criteria (e.g., AIC BIC). information available criteria,          see modelCriterion function documentation.         - using numeric vector, ensure order lag values matches variables formula.         - using named vectors, double-check variable names avoid mismatches unintended results.         - mode bypasses automatic lag optimization assumes user-defined lags correct. `mode` parameter provides flexibility different use cases:        - Use `"grid"` mode debugging interactive use progress visibility important.        - Use `"grid_custom"` mode minimize overhead computationally intensive tasks.        - Specify user-defined vector customize lag structure based prior knowledge analysis. Selecting appropriate mode can improve efficiency usability function depending        user's requirements computational environment. criterion string specifying information criterion used selecting optimal lag structure.       available options : "AIC": Akaike Information Criterion (default). criterion balances model fit complexity,       favoring models explain data well fewer parameters. "BIC": Bayesian Information Criterion. criterion imposes stronger penalty model complexity       AIC, making conservative selecting models fewer parameters. "AICc": Corrected Akaike Information Criterion. adjusted version AIC accounts small sample sizes,       making suitable number observations limited relative number parameters. text: object class kardl_lm containing estimated ARDL NARDL model. object includes following components: argsInfo list input arguments used estimation. includes data, formula, maxlag, mode, criterion, differentAsymLag, batch settings. extractedInfo list containing extracted information input data formula, variable names, deterministic terms, asymmetric variables, prepared dataset estimation. timeInfo list containing timing information estimation process, including start time, end time, total duration. lagInfo list containing lag selection information, including optimal lag configuration criteria values different lag combinations. estInfo list containing estimation details, type model, estimation method, model formula, number parameters (k), number observations (n), start end points fitted values, total time span. model fitted linear model object class lm representing estimated ARDL NARDL model.

title: Estimate ARDL and NARDL Models with Automatic Lag Selection — kardl
text: general formula long-run model specified follows: $$ \begin{aligned}  \Delta {y}_t =  c  +  \eta _0   {y}_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} { \gamma_{j}  \Delta {y}_{t-j} }   +\sum_{=1}^{m} {( \eta ^{+}_i   {x}^{+}_{,t-1 } + \eta ^{-}_i   {x}^{-}_{,t-1 } ) }    +  \sum_{=m+1}^{k} {\eta _i   {x}_{,t-1 } } +  \newline  \sum_{=1}^{m} {\sum_{j=0}^{q^+_i} { \beta^+_{ij}   \Delta {x}^+_{,t-j} } } + \sum_{=1}^{m} {\sum_{j=0}^{q^-_i} { \beta^-_{ij}   \Delta {x}^-_{,t-j} } }  + \sum_{=m+1}^{k} {\sum_{j=0}^{q_i} { \beta_{ij}   \Delta {x}_{,t-j} } }+ e_t \end{aligned}  $$ : \(y_t\) dependent variable time t. \(c\) constant term. \(\eta_0\) coefficient lagged dependent variable. \(\gamma_j\) coefficients lagged differences dependent variable. \(\eta^+_i\) \(\eta^-_i\) coefficients positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(\eta_i\) coefficients independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(\beta^+_{ij}\) \(\beta^-_{ij}\) coefficients lagged differences positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(\beta_{ij}\) coefficients lagged differences independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(e_t\) error term time t. \(p\) maximum lag length dependent variable. \(q^+_i\) \(q^-_i\) maximum lag lengths positive negative decompositions independent variables, respectively. \(q_i\) maximum lag length independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(m\) number independent variables asymmetric decompositions. \(k\) total number independent variables. \(\Delta\) denotes first difference operator. \(x^+_{,t}\) \(x^-_{,t}\) represent positive negative decompositions independent variable \(x_i\) time t, respectively.

title: Estimate ARDL and NARDL Models with Automatic Lag Selection — kardl
text: reported coefficients, prefix L denotes lagged variables, accompanying number indicates lag order, .d. denotes first differences. Accordingly, L1.PPI represents first lag level PPI (long-run component), L3.d.PPI denotes third lag first-differenced PPI (short-run component). addition, suffixes _POS _NEG indicate positive negative partial sum components variable, respectively. notation used default remains valid unless modified kardl_set() function.

title: Compute Long-Run Multipliers from a kardl Model — kardl_longrun
text: object class kardl_long_run, list containing: coefficients: named vector long-run multipliers. residuals: vector residuals long-run model. effects: vector effects long-run model. rank: rank long-run model. fitted.values: vector fitted values long-run model. assign: vector indicating assignment coefficients terms long-run model. qr: QR decomposition design matrix long-run model. df.residual: degrees freedom residuals long-run model. xlevels: list factor levels used long-run model. call: matched call used create long-run model. terms: terms object long-run model. model: data frame used long-run model.

title: Compute Long-Run Multipliers from a kardl Model — kardl_longrun
text: function also calculates standard errors long-run multipliers using delta method, accounts covariance coefficients. fitted values residuals long-run model calculated based original data long-run multipliers. function returns object class kardl_long_run, contains long-run multipliers, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, fitted values, residuals, relevant information analysis diagnostics. Note fitted values residuals long-run model centered (.e., mean zero) design, means diagnostic plots residual-based tests may valid model. primary focus function estimation long-run multipliers associated statistics. long-run multipliers calculated using formula: $$LRM_i = -\frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0}$$. t-values p-values calculated using standard errors obtained delta method, accounts covariance coefficients. Delta method formula standard errors long-run multipliers: $$SE(LR_i) = \sqrt{(^2) \cdot Var(\eta_i) + 2 \cdot \cdot B \cdot Cov(\eta_i, \eta_0) + (B^2) \cdot Var(\eta_0)}$$ $$= \frac{\partial LRM_i}{\partial \eta_i} = -\frac{1}{\eta_0}$$ $$B = \frac{\partial LRM_i}{\partial \eta_0} = \frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0^2}$$ . Hence, \(\eta_i\) coefficient independent variable \(\eta_0\) coefficient dependent variable original KARDL model.

title: Compute Long-Run Multipliers from a kardl Model — kardl_longrun

title: Model Selection Criteria — modelCriterion
text: function returns model selection criteria used compare quality different models. criteria defined lower values indicate better models (.e., goal minimization). wish compare models using maximization approach (e.g., log-likelihood), can multiply result -1. Note: predefined string options (e.g., "AIC") built-R functions AIC() BIC(). particular, values returned function adjusted dividing sample size n (.e., normalized AIC/BIC), makes comparable across datasets different sizes. function returns: "AIC":  \( \frac{2k - 2\ell}{n} \) Akaike Information Criterion divided n. "BIC":  \( \frac{\log(n) \cdot k - 2\ell}{n} \) Bayesian Information Criterion divided n. "AICc": \( \frac{2k(k+1)}{n - k - 1} + \frac{2k - 2\ell}{n} \) Corrected Akaike Information Criterion divided n. "HQ": \( \frac{2 \log(\log(n)) \cdot k - 2\ell}{n} \) Hannan-Quinn Criterion divided n. : \(k\) number parameters, \(n\) sample size, \(\ell\) log-likelihood model. cr function, called fitted model additional arguments passed ....

title: Compute Dynamic Multipliers for kardl Models — mplier
text: asymmetry structure determined internally: Variables extractedInfo$ASvars treated asymmetric short run. Variables extractedInfo$ALvars treated asymmetric long run. allows four possible configurations: LL: Linear short-run long-run NN: Asymmetric short-run long-run SA: Short-run linear, long-run asymmetric : Short-run asymmetric, long-run linear component linear, coefficient path used positive negative changes. asymmetric, separate positive negative effects computed. mplier function computes dynamic multipliers based coefficients lag structure model estimated using kardl package. function extracts necessary information model, coefficients, lag structure, variable names, compute dynamic multipliers. calculates short-run coefficients, Lambda values, omega values based model's parameters lag structure. output includes matrix dynamic multipliers (mpsi), can used analysis visualization. dynamic multipliers provide insight changes independent variables affect dependent variable time, allowing deeper understanding relationships captured model. function also allows users set minimum p-value threshold including coefficients calculation, providing flexibility focusing statistically significant effects. function constructs dynamic multipliers based recursive relationship: $$ \psi_{h}^{+} = \sum_{=0}^{h} \frac{\partial y_{t+}}{\partial x_{t}^{+}}, \quad \psi_{h}^{-} = \sum_{=0}^{h} \frac{\partial y_{t+}}{\partial x_{t}^{-}} $$ \(\psi_h^{+}\) \(\psi_h^{-}\) represent cumulative responses positive negative shocks. recursion defined : $$ \psi_h = \lambda_h + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \omega_j \psi_{h-j} $$ \(\lambda_h\) captures short-run effects \(\omega_j\) reflects persistence lagged dependent variables. asymmetry present, positive negative shocks propagated separately. Otherwise, dynamic path used.

title: Compute Dynamic Multipliers for kardl Models — mplier
code: # This example demonstrates how to use the mplier function to calculate dynamic multipliers # from a model estimated using the kardl package. The example includes fitting a model with # the kardl function, calculating the multipliers, and visualizing the results using both # base R plotting and ggplot2.   # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a linear model in short and long run (NN)  kardl_model<-kardl(imf_example_data, CPI~ER )  m<-mplier(kardl_model,40)  head(m$mpsi) #>      h    ER_POS     ER_NEG ER_dif #> [1,] 0 0.1011301 -0.1011301      0 #> [2,] 1 0.2430853 -0.2430853      0 #> [3,] 2 0.3149552 -0.3149552      0 #> [4,] 3 0.3564520 -0.3564520      0 #> [5,] 4 0.3891548 -0.3891548      0 #> [6,] 5 0.4154632 -0.4154632      0  plot(m)    # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with  # Short-run linear, long-run asymmetric (SA)  kardl_model<-kardl(imf_example_data, CPI~lasym(ER) )  m<-mplier(kardl_model,40)  head(m$mpsi) #>      h    ER_POS     ER_NEG ER_dif #> [1,] 0 0.1016347 -0.1016347      0 #> [2,] 1 0.2438260 -0.2438260      0 #> [3,] 2 0.3149033 -0.3149033      0 #> [4,] 3 0.3546481 -0.3546481      0 #> [5,] 4 0.3853433 -0.3853433      0 #> [6,] 5 0.4103829 -0.4103829      0  plot(m)    # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with  # Short-run asymmetric, long-run linear (AS)  kardl_model<-kardl(imf_example_data, CPI~sasym(ER) )  m<-mplier(kardl_model,40)  head(m$mpsi) #>      h    ER_POS      ER_NEG     ER_dif #> [1,] 0 0.1168062 -0.02760744 0.08919873 #> [2,] 1 0.2613810 -0.04554608 0.21583495 #> [3,] 2 0.3275027 -0.04989274 0.27760999 #> [4,] 3 0.3633209 -0.03618489 0.32713603 #> [5,] 4 0.3930679 -0.05739910 0.33566883 #> [6,] 5 0.4172614 -0.01462712 0.40263432  plot(m)    # Calculating dynamic multipliers for a model with  # asymmetric effects in both short and long run (NN)  kardl_model<-kardl(imf_example_data, CPI~asym(ER) )  m<-mplier(kardl_model,40)  head(m$mpsi) #>      h    ER_POS      ER_NEG      ER_dif #> [1,] 0 0.1148948 -0.03765414  0.07724068 #> [2,] 1 0.2574646 -0.13534259  0.12212202 #> [3,] 2 0.3218792 -0.24609073  0.07578847 #> [4,] 3 0.3553747 -0.34526280  0.01011187 #> [5,] 4 0.3828353 -0.48437435 -0.10153903 #> [6,] 5 0.4058231 -0.55248000 -0.14665691  plot(m)    # The mpsi matrix contains the cumulative dynamic multipliers for each variable and time horizon.  # The omega vector contains the persistence structure of the model,  # while the lambda matrix contains the short-run dynamic coefficients.  # You can inspect these components to understand the dynamics captured by the model.  kardl_model<-kardl(imf_example_data, CPI~PPI+asym(ER) )  m<-mplier(kardl_model,40)  head(m$mpsi) #>      h    PPI_POS     PPI_NEG PPI_dif    ER_POS      ER_NEG     ER_dif #> [1,] 0 0.02132813 -0.02132813       0 0.1063165 -0.03176480 0.07455172 #> [2,] 1 0.07315202 -0.07315202       0 0.2435925 -0.08346931 0.16012314 #> [3,] 2 0.11052625 -0.11052625       0 0.3036652 -0.13943921 0.16422600 #> [4,] 3 0.14464258 -0.14464258       0 0.3292762 -0.19429402 0.13498218 #> [5,] 4 0.20964369 -0.20964369       0 0.3467835 -0.24716862 0.09961483 #> [6,] 5 0.28576074 -0.28576074       0 0.3634339 -0.29819112 0.06524275  head(m$omega) #> [1]  1.37561845 -0.47712514  0.07968277  head(m$lambda) #>           PPI_POS      PPI_NEG       ER_POS      ER_NEG #> [1,]  0.021328127  0.021328127  0.106316519 0.031764799 #> [2,]  0.022484531  0.022484531 -0.008975032 0.008008269 #> [3,] -0.023739497 -0.023739497 -0.078040265 0.000000000 #> [4,]  0.005730656  0.005730656  0.000000000 0.000000000 #> [5,]  0.031772768  0.031772768  0.000000000 0.000000000 #> [6,]  0.000000000  0.000000000  0.000000000 0.000000000   # title: Narayan Bounds Test — narayan
text: function performs Narayan test, designed assess cointegration using critical values specifically tailored small sample sizes. Unlike traditional cointegration tests may rely asymptotic distributions, Narayan test adjusts limitations small samples, providing accurate results contexts. makes test particularly useful studies fewer observations, accounts sample size constraints determining presence long-term equilibrium relationship variables.

title: Pesaran, Shin, and Smith t Bounds Test — psst

Updated example structures atomic assignment-based, improving automated test generation reproducibility. Print method improvements: Standard output handling print methods revised. Documentation fixes: Corrected Rd syntax issues mismatched braces, non-ASCII characters, inconsistent list environments (\itemize{} vs \describe{}). Improved clarity consistency across help pages. Examples refactored: Rewrote examples avoid fragile constructs chained pipelines (%>%) without proper imports, standalone expressions, state-dependent workflows. Examples now follow minimal deterministic structure. Function interface refinement: Updated function signatures (e.g., removal nonstandard argument names .) improve compatibility automated tools standard R practices. Dependency handling: Improved handling suggested packages examples using @examplesIf, ensuring optional dependencies (e.g., magrittr, dplyr) break checks. Minor fixes cleanup: General code cleanup, improved formatting, consistency updates across package.
